paragraphs.body: Cold cases are known for the mystery and intrigue that surround the case at hand. The constant wondering about who did it? Will this case ever be solved? Will the victim, family, and loved ones ever receive closure? Constant questions that are always circulating about these cases that have gone unsolved. One in particular personally piques my interest the most. The case about Elizabeth Short or more popularly known as “The Black Dahlia.”

paragraphs.body: In the podcast, Cold Case Files, narrated by Brooke Gittings, she released an episode titled, “The Black Dahlia.” In this episode Gittings reminds us about the unsolved cold case of a young and upcoming actor in Hollywood, Elizabeth Short. She supports her point by developing several important ideas. First, in the podcast Gittings begins by acknowledging the explicit details that surrounded Elizabeth Short's murder including her life leading up to the murder. For instance, the mutilation of her body as well as the humiliation of her body by explaining the location and way she was posed that was involved in her murder. Gittings also gave her listeners insight to the kind of person Elizabeth Short was before she was brutally killed and staged. Next, she advocates for the theories surrounding the upcoming actress’s death and the list of potential suspects. Gittings accomplishes this by bringing attention to the most supported theories that are backed with evidence from the case, and the most likely suspects that have been looked at by multiple detectives. Finally, she encourages the listeners of the podcast to make their own conclusions. She allows for this to be done from the details she went over in the episode.

annotations.text: In this episode Gittings reminds us about the unsolved cold case of a young and upcoming actor in Hollywood, Elizabeth Short.
annotations.comment: Nice job with summary templates/moves and signal verbs throughout this paragraph :)

paragraphs.body: Elizabeth Short was a beautiful up and coming girl in California, so the question is why is it that she was murdered? As was mentioned earlier, the questions are racing through my mind. After listening to the podcast, I was able to come up with my own conclusions about who killed her. I believe that George Hodel was Elizabeth’s killer. The facts presented in the podcast led me to that inquiry. Gittings presented in the episode that homicide detective Steve Hodel suspected his father George Hodel to be the killer. According to Gittings,” Steve believes his father murdered Elizabeth Short.” Gittings point is that George Hodel is a good candidate for being the one to have murdered Elizabeth. Afterwards, the quote was followed up with details as to why he is a good candidate. She goes on to say how there were photos found of a girl who looked just like Elizabeth in his father’s things and his father was also a doctor. George Hodel being a doctor incriminates him even more since the autopsy proved that the cuts Elizabeth received were done by a professional and most likely a doctor. Elizabeth has a rough life growing up and leading to her death. She suffered from asthma, no father in the picture, living through the great depression, trouble with the police, bounced from relationship to relationship, and was known to lie. However, she still was determined to make something of herself and to be known. Just not in the way she thought.

paragraphs.body: After I first learned about cold cases I became obsessed. I have always been a sucker for true crime including, the television shows, documentaries, and the videos that popped up while scrolling through TikTok. When I first learned about “The Black Dahlia” I became intensely interested and involved. I didn’t know why I was enthralled with this cold case, but I was and ended up reading and watching anything I could about her murder. Now, this was years ago when I first learned about Elizabeth Short. However, I rediscovered this murder case once again. I was at first wondering as to why I was so interested and drawn in until I made my connection. This may be an odd connection one might make, but I now understand.

I never finish anything if I can help it. Books, a new craft, or a television series. There will always be a chapter left in any book I read for my own enjoyment, one part left to finish in a craft, and one more episode to watch. I didn’t know why I did this until I began going to therapy for my seasonal depression. My therapist and I would have conversation after conversation that seemed to go nowhere. That was until I began opening up and she was now asking different questions. She started to notice my pattern about not finishing things when I was so close to it. We spent session after session trying to crack this code. Then it happened. She asked the one question I didn’t want to address which related to my younger years as a child. My mother was an alcoholic because of her sister's death, my aunt, when I was six years old until I turned eight. I don’t have a lot of memories of that time other than some pretty upsetting ones I choose to ignore. My therapist and I broke it down and she helped me come to the conclusion that I never finish anything if I can help it because deep down, I never got the treatment I deserved as a kid and I feel unfinished at that time of my life. It was eye opening and weird that was the case, and then we came up with how to heal from it and I am still working on that. My mother is and has been recovered for multiple years now and is the best mom you could ever want.

paragraphs.body: I associate feeling unfinished in my childhood as my own personal cold case. I think that is why I am so enthralled and obsessed with cold cases because the ones I am intrigued the most don’t have an ending. Furthermore, the Elizabeth Short cold case is my favorite, and I now know why and that is I can relate to Elizabeth in some ways. While her father faked his suicide and left her for some time, he then came back into her life she found her way to him. Gittings reports,”

She discovered her father was very much alive and living in California.” That quote made me feel that in a way, my mother died the day her sister died and then came back to life after rehab. I think Elizabeth and I share that common factor of missing a parent when we needed them most. I am happy to see that I didn’t make the same choices as Elizabeth. Now, I know that we had very different upbringings and lives, but anyone who deals with an absent parent is prone to acting out and bad behavior. I chose the route of good behavior and to make the right choices. I think Elizabeth had outlandish behavior because of her father and that it led her to make the wrong choices. I will never regret my childhood and what I went through because it made me who I am today and maybe one day I can find the solution to my personal cold case. PodcastOne. PodcastOne: The Black Dahlia. https://www.podcastone.com/episode/The-Black-Dahlia.
